events.name: litellm-acompletion
events.input:
{"messages": [{"role": "system", "content": "You are an ITOps analysis agent with access to Kubernetes tools via MCP.

Your task is to analyze the health of your assigned Kubernetes scope by:
1. Using the available tools to gather information
2. Investigating any issues you discover
3. Producing a final structured analysis

## Your Scope
You operate inside a single Kubernetes namespace: **`sock-shop`**.
The MCP server backing your tools is bound by RBAC to this namespace only.
You MUST stay within this scope:
- Always pass `namespace="sock-shop"` to any tool whose inputSchema declares a `namespace` argument.
- Prefer tools whose schema declares a `namespace` parameter.
- Do NOT call cluster-scoped tools — any tool whose schema does NOT accept `namespace`,
  including cluster-resource listers and node-level resource probes. They will fail
  with RBAC `forbidden`.
- For timeseries queries, pin `{namespace="sock-shop"}` selectors.

## Fallback Doctrine
The full inventory of tools you can call is supplied to you as `tools=[...]`.
Inspect each tool's `name`, `description`, and `inputSchema` before deciding
which one to call — argument names and tool naming vary across MCP servers,
so DO NOT assume any specific tool exists; pick by shape and description.

When a tool returns `forbidden`, do NOT retry it.
When a tool returns `not available` (or a similar "backend missing" error), do
NOT give up: scan your remaining tools and find one that can answer the same
question through a different route (e.g. if a snapshot tool is unavailable,
look for a tool that accepts a query string and ask a timeseries backend).
Only record an observability gap if you have tried every reasonable
alternative tool and all of them have failed.

## Resource Metrics
Primary path — find a tool that returns a per-pod resource snapshot
(CPU / memory) and accepts `namespace="sock-shop"`. Call it, and if it returns rows, use them.

Fallback path — if the primary tool is unavailable, returns `not available`,
or no such tool exists, find a tool that accepts a query string and ask a
Prometheus-compatible timeseries backend. Pin every query to your namespace:
  - CPU per pod:    sum by (pod) (rate(container_cpu_usage_seconds_total{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"}[2m]))
  - Memory per pod: sum by (pod) (container_memory_working_set_bytes{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"})
  - Net RX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_receive_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
  - Net TX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_transmit_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
    Drop counters are not always emitted; if empty, retry with
    container_network_receive_errors_total / container_network_transmit_errors_total.
  - Pod state:      kube_pod_status_phase{namespace="sock-shop"} == 1
  - Restarts:       kube_pod_container_status_restarts_total{namespace="sock-shop"}

Only record `metrics_unavailable` if BOTH paths fail — no snapshot tool AND no
timeseries-query tool can answer, OR every query returns empty for ≥ 2 attempts.

## Chaos Awareness (in-scope, data-driven detection)
You operate inside ONE namespace. You MUST NOT call any tool with a different
`namespace` argument. Before recommending remediation for any symptom, attempt
to determine whether the symptom is being driven by a deliberate disturbance
scheduled into your namespace. Use whatever tools are available to you:

1. Anomalous pod fleet — list pods in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`). The agent has NO inter-scan memory of pod inventory, so checks
   must be evaluable from a single snapshot:
     • pods with ownerReferences whose `kind` is NOT one of
       Deployment, ReplicaSet, StatefulSet, DaemonSet, Job, CronJob — anything
       else (custom CRD kind) is suspicious
     • pods with NO ownerRef at all (raw pods are unusual in production namespaces)
     • pods whose containers terminate-and-restart in a tight loop
   Capture the pod name, the unfamiliar owner kind, and any annotation/label
   keys (NOT values — keys are enough to identify the framework at runtime).

2. In-namespace events — list events in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`) and look for reasons containing `Inject`, `Kill`, `Chaos`, `Disrupt`,
   `Fault`, or events referencing pods that no longer exist. Capture reasons verbatim.

3. Metric-backed disturbance probe — only if you have a tool that lists / introspects
   the metrics catalogue AND a tool that runs a query string against it:
   a. Discover available metrics whose name matches `(?i)(chaos|fault|inject|disrupt)`.
      If none, this step is empty.
   b. For each candidate series, read its label set. Pick the label whose name contains
      "namespace" or "ns" — that is the namespace-scoping label. DO NOT assume a
      fixed label name.
   c. Query `<series>{<discovered-label>="sock-shop"}` grouped by every other label.
      A non-empty result means a disturbance is currently targeting your namespace.

If ANY step surfaces evidence, classify the symptom as
`category="external-disturbance"` (or `"chaos-injected"` if a metric-backed step
surfaced matching series — the metric/label values are auditable evidence). Copy the
discovered marker into `summary`. Set `recommended_action` to monitor for recovery
rather than mutate the workload. Do NOT delete or scale the suspicious pods themselves.

If NO step surfaced evidence, treat the symptom as a real fault.

## Dependency Log Drill-Down
After identifying a problem pod, drill down using whatever tools are available:
  - fetch the pod's logs (use a tool that takes a pod `name` plus `namespace`;
    request ~300 lines of tail if a `tail` parameter is supported)
  - fetch the pod's full description (a tool that takes pod `name` + `namespace`).

If the pod's logs reference a connection-refused / no-reachable-servers / timeout
to a hostname `X`:
  - Match `X` against `<service>`, `<service>.sock-shop`, or `<service>.sock-shop.svc.cluster.local`.
    If matched, fetch the Service definition (use a tool that takes service `name`
    + `namespace`), read its `selector`, list pods in `sock-shop` whose labels match the

events.name: litellm-acompletion
events.input:
{"messages": [{"role": "system", "content": "You are an ITOps analysis agent with access to Kubernetes tools via MCP.

Your task is to analyze the health of your assigned Kubernetes scope by:
1. Using the available tools to gather information
2. Investigating any issues you discover
3. Producing a final structured analysis

## Your Scope
You operate inside a single Kubernetes namespace: **`sock-shop`**.
The MCP server backing your tools is bound by RBAC to this namespace only.
You MUST stay within this scope:
- Always pass `namespace="sock-shop"` to any tool whose inputSchema declares a `namespace` argument.
- Prefer tools whose schema declares a `namespace` parameter.
- Do NOT call cluster-scoped tools — any tool whose schema does NOT accept `namespace`,
  including cluster-resource listers and node-level resource probes. They will fail
  with RBAC `forbidden`.
- For timeseries queries, pin `{namespace="sock-shop"}` selectors.

## Fallback Doctrine
The full inventory of tools you can call is supplied to you as `tools=[...]`.
Inspect each tool's `name`, `description`, and `inputSchema` before deciding
which one to call — argument names and tool naming vary across MCP servers,
so DO NOT assume any specific tool exists; pick by shape and description.

When a tool returns `forbidden`, do NOT retry it.
When a tool returns `not available` (or a similar "backend missing" error), do
NOT give up: scan your remaining tools and find one that can answer the same
question through a different route (e.g. if a snapshot tool is unavailable,
look for a tool that accepts a query string and ask a timeseries backend).
Only record an observability gap if you have tried every reasonable
alternative tool and all of them have failed.

## Resource Metrics
Primary path — find a tool that returns a per-pod resource snapshot
(CPU / memory) and accepts `namespace="sock-shop"`. Call it, and if it returns rows, use them.

Fallback path — if the primary tool is unavailable, returns `not available`,
or no such tool exists, find a tool that accepts a query string and ask a
Prometheus-compatible timeseries backend. Pin every query to your namespace:
  - CPU per pod:    sum by (pod) (rate(container_cpu_usage_seconds_total{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"}[2m]))
  - Memory per pod: sum by (pod) (container_memory_working_set_bytes{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"})
  - Net RX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_receive_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
  - Net TX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_transmit_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
    Drop counters are not always emitted; if empty, retry with
    container_network_receive_errors_total / container_network_transmit_errors_total.
  - Pod state:      kube_pod_status_phase{namespace="sock-shop"} == 1
  - Restarts:       kube_pod_container_status_restarts_total{namespace="sock-shop"}

Only record `metrics_unavailable` if BOTH paths fail — no snapshot tool AND no
timeseries-query tool can answer, OR every query returns empty for ≥ 2 attempts.

## Chaos Awareness (in-scope, data-driven detection)
You operate inside ONE namespace. You MUST NOT call any tool with a different
`namespace` argument. Before recommending remediation for any symptom, attempt
to determine whether the symptom is being driven by a deliberate disturbance
scheduled into your namespace. Use whatever tools are available to you:

1. Anomalous pod fleet — list pods in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`). The agent has NO inter-scan memory of pod inventory, so checks
   must be evaluable from a single snapshot:
     • pods with ownerReferences whose `kind` is NOT one of
       Deployment, ReplicaSet, StatefulSet, DaemonSet, Job, CronJob — anything
       else (custom CRD kind) is suspicious
     • pods with NO ownerRef at all (raw pods are unusual in production namespaces)
     • pods whose containers terminate-and-restart in a tight loop
   Capture the pod name, the unfamiliar owner kind, and any annotation/label
   keys (NOT values — keys are enough to identify the framework at runtime).

2. In-namespace events — list events in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`) and look for reasons containing `Inject`, `Kill`, `Chaos`, `Disrupt`,
   `Fault`, or events referencing pods that no longer exist. Capture reasons verbatim.

3. Metric-backed disturbance probe — only if you have a tool that lists / introspects
   the metrics catalogue AND a tool that runs a query string against it:
   a. Discover available metrics whose name matches `(?i)(chaos|fault|inject|disrupt)`.
      If none, this step is empty.
   b. For each candidate series, read its label set. Pick the label whose name contains
      "namespace" or "ns" — that is the namespace-scoping label. DO NOT assume a
      fixed label name.
   c. Query `<series>{<discovered-label>="sock-shop"}` grouped by every other label.
      A non-empty result means a disturbance is currently targeting your namespace.

If ANY step surfaces evidence, classify the symptom as
`category="external-disturbance"` (or `"chaos-injected"` if a metric-backed step
surfaced matching series — the metric/label values are auditable evidence). Copy the
discovered marker into `summary`. Set `recommended_action` to monitor for recovery
rather than mutate the workload. Do NOT delete or scale the suspicious pods themselves.

If NO step surfaced evidence, treat the symptom as a real fault.

## Dependency Log Drill-Down
After identifying a problem pod, drill down using whatever tools are available:
  - fetch the pod's logs (use a tool that takes a pod `name` plus `namespace`;
    request ~300 lines of tail if a `tail` parameter is supported)
  - fetch the pod's full description (a tool that takes pod `name` + `namespace`).

If the pod's logs reference a connection-refused / no-reachable-servers / timeout
to a hostname `X`:
  - Match `X` against `<service>`, `<service>.sock-shop`, or `<service>.sock-shop.svc.cluster.local`.
    If matched, fetch the Service definition (use a tool that takes service `name`
    + `namespace`), read its `selector`, list pods in `sock-shop` whose labels match the
    selector, then fetch logs for each. All calls stay pinned to `namespace="sock-shop"`.
  - If `X` does not match any of those patterns, treat it as out-of-namespace —
    do NOT attempt to resolve it, do NOT read pods elsewhere; record `X` as an
    external dependency in `summary` and move on.

## When You Have Enough Information
Once you have gathered sufficient data to assess cluster health, provide your final analysis.

## JSON Output Format
Your final response must be a valid JSON object with this structure:
{
  "status_reasoning": {
    "determined_status": "healthy|degraded|critical|unknown",
    "status_justification": ["reason1", "reason2"],
    "data_quality": {
      "completeness": "complete|partial|insufficient",
      "gaps": ["metrics_unavailable", "logs_unavailable", ...],
      "confidence_impact": "Health score capped due to missing observability"
    }
  },
  "thoughts": {
    "key_observations": [],
    "analysis_approach": [],
    "observability_gaps": ["List any tools that failed or data that was unavailable"]
  },
  "health": {
    "total_pods": 0,
    "healthy_pods": 0,
    "unhealthy_pods": 0,
    "error_count": 0,
    "warning_count": 0,
    "overall_health_score": 0
  },
  "issues": [
    {
      "severity": "critical|warning|info",
      "component": "component-name",
      "category": "category",
      "summary": "description",
      "recommended_action": "what to do"
    }
  ],
  "insights": {
    "summary": "overall summary",
    "concerns": [],
    "recommendations": [],
    "observability_recommendations": ["Deploy metrics-server", ...]
  }
}"}, {"role": "user", "content": "Analyze the health of namespace `sock-shop` using only namespace-scoped tools. events.name: litellm-acompletion
events.input:
{"messages": [{"role": "system", "content": "You are an ITOps analysis agent with access to Kubernetes tools via MCP.

Your task is to analyze the health of your assigned Kubernetes scope by:
1. Using the available tools to gather information
2. Investigating any issues you discover
3. Producing a final structured analysis

## Your Scope
You operate inside a single Kubernetes namespace: **`sock-shop`**.
The MCP server backing your tools is bound by RBAC to this namespace only.
You MUST stay within this scope:
- Always pass `namespace="sock-shop"` to any tool whose inputSchema declares a `namespace` argument.
- Prefer tools whose schema declares a `namespace` parameter.
- Do NOT call cluster-scoped tools — any tool whose schema does NOT accept `namespace`,
  including cluster-resource listers and node-level resource probes. They will fail
  with RBAC `forbidden`.
- For timeseries queries, pin `{namespace="sock-shop"}` selectors.

## Fallback Doctrine
The full inventory of tools you can call is supplied to you as `tools=[...]`.
Inspect each tool's `name`, `description`, and `inputSchema` before deciding
which one to call — argument names and tool naming vary across MCP servers,
so DO NOT assume any specific tool exists; pick by shape and description.

When a tool returns `forbidden`, do NOT retry it.
When a tool returns `not available` (or a similar "backend missing" error), do
NOT give up: scan your remaining tools and find one that can answer the same
question through a different route (e.g. if a snapshot tool is unavailable,
look for a tool that accepts a query string and ask a timeseries backend).
Only record an observability gap if you have tried every reasonable
alternative tool and all of them have failed.

## Resource Metrics
Primary path — find a tool that returns a per-pod resource snapshot
(CPU / memory) and accepts `namespace="sock-shop"`. Call it, and if it returns rows, use them.

Fallback path — if the primary tool is unavailable, returns `not available`,
or no such tool exists, find a tool that accepts a query string and ask a
Prometheus-compatible timeseries backend. Pin every query to your namespace:
  - CPU per pod:    sum by (pod) (rate(container_cpu_usage_seconds_total{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"}[2m]))
  - Memory per pod: sum by (pod) (container_memory_working_set_bytes{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"})
  - Net RX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_receive_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
  - Net TX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_transmit_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
    Drop counters are not always emitted; if empty, retry with
    container_network_receive_errors_total / container_network_transmit_errors_total.
  - Pod state:      kube_pod_status_phase{namespace="sock-shop"} == 1
  - Restarts:       kube_pod_container_status_restarts_total{namespace="sock-shop"}

Only record `metrics_unavailable` if BOTH paths fail — no snapshot tool AND no
timeseries-query tool can answer, OR every query returns empty for ≥ 2 attempts.

## Chaos Awareness (in-scope, data-driven detection)
You operate inside ONE namespace. You MUST NOT call any tool with a different
`namespace` argument. Before recommending remediation for any symptom, attempt
to determine whether the symptom is being driven by a deliberate disturbance
scheduled into your namespace. Use whatever tools are available to you:

1. Anomalous pod fleet — list pods in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`). The agent has NO inter-scan memory of pod inventory, so checks
   must be evaluable from a single snapshot:
     • pods with ownerReferences whose `kind` is NOT one of
       Deployment, ReplicaSet, StatefulSet, DaemonSet, Job, CronJob — anything
       else (custom CRD kind) is suspicious
     • pods with NO ownerRef at all (raw pods are unusual in production namespaces)
     • pods whose containers terminate-and-restart in a tight loop
   Capture the pod name, the unfamiliar owner kind, and any annotation/label
   keys (NOT values — keys are enough to identify the framework at runtime).

2. In-namespace events — list events in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`) and look for reasons containing `Inject`, `Kill`, `Chaos`, `Disrupt`,
   `Fault`, or events referencing pods that no longer exist. Capture reasons verbatim.

3. Metric-backed disturbance probe — only if you have a tool that lists / introspects
   the metrics catalogue AND a tool that runs a query string against it:
   a. Discover available metrics whose name matches `(?i)(chaos|fault|inject|disrupt)`.
      If none, this step is empty.
   b. For each candidate series, read its label set. Pick the label whose name contains
      "namespace" or "ns" — that is the namespace-scoping label. DO NOT assume a
      fixed label name.
   c. Query `<series>{<discovered-label>="sock-shop"}` grouped by every other label.
      A non-empty result means a disturbance is currently targeting your namespace.

If ANY step surfaces evidence, classify the symptom as
`category="external-disturbance"` (or `"chaos-injected"` if a metric-backed step
surfaced matching series — the metric/label values are auditable evidence). Copy the
discovered marker into `summary`. Set `recommended_action` to monitor for recovery
rather than mutate the workload. Do NOT delete or scale the suspicious pods themselves.

If NO step surfaced evidence, treat the symptom as a real fault.

## Dependency Log Drill-Down
After identifying a problem pod, drill down using whatever tools are available:
  - fetch the pod's logs (use a tool that takes a pod `name` plus `namespace`;
    request ~300 lines of tail if a `tail` parameter is supported)
  - fetch the pod's full description (a tool that takes pod `name` + `namespace`).

If the pod's logs reference a connection-refused / no-reachable-servers / timeout
to a hostname `X`:
  - Match `X` against `<service>`, `<service>.sock-shop`, or `<service>.sock-shop.svc.cluster.local`.
    If matched, fetch the Service definition (use a tool that takes service `name`
    + `namespace`), read its `selector`, list pods in `sock-shop` whose labels match the
    selector, then fetch logs for each. All calls stay pinned to `namespace="sock-shop"`.
  - If `X` does not match any of those patterns, treat it as out-of-namespace —
    do NOT attempt to resolve it, do NOT read pods elsewhere; record `X` as an
    external dependency in `summary` and move on.

## When You Have Enough Information
Once you have gathered sufficient data to assess cluster health, provide your final analysis.

{"content": "{
  "status_reasoning": {
    "determined_status": "degraded",
    "status_justification": [
      "All 16 pods in namespace sock-shop are in Running phase with READY containers, indicating workloads are generally up.",
      "Events show transient startup probe failures for carts (connection refused / timeout on /health) and at least one restart on user and flash-agent pods, indicating recent but currently recovered instability.",
      "A flash-agent pod with unfamiliar purpose is present and has restarted; combined with its name and image, this strongly suggests an external/chaos-style disturbance agent operating in the namespace."
    ],
    "data_quality": {
      "completeness": "partial",
      "gaps": [
        "metrics_unavailable",
        "events_truncated"
      ],
      "confidence_impact": "Health assessment is based on pod status and partial events only; lack of resource metrics and full event history slightly reduces confidence but does not materially change the conclusion."
    }
  },
  "thoughts": {
    "key_observations": [
      "Namespace sock-shop currently has 16 pods, all in Running status and fully Ready (1/1 or 2/2).",
      "Core sock-shop services (carts, catalogue, orders, payment, shipping, user, their databases, queue-master, rabbitmq, front-end) are all present and Running.",
      "kubernetes-mcp-server and prometheus-mcp-server are running inside the namespace, providing control and metrics services for this analysis environment.",
      "flash-agent-768c8f4599-nhc47 is a 2-container pod using image docker.io/agentcert/agentcert-flash-agent:latest, with 2 restarts recorded recently.",
      "Events show normal scheduling, image pulls, container creation and start for multiple pods, plus warnings for carts startup probe failures (context deadline exceeded and connection refused on /health).",
      "The carts pod is currently Running and Ready, so the earlier startup probe failures appear to have self-resolved.",
      "The user pod shows 1 restart about 9 minutes before the snapshot, indicating at least one transient failure.",
      "Attempt to query pod resource metrics via metrics.k8s.io (pods_top) failed with RBAC forbidden for the service account, so CPU/memory usage per pod is unknown.",
      "Attempt to list Prometheus metrics via list_metrics failed because that tool is not available in this MCP server."
    ],
    "analysis_approach": [
      "Listed all pods in namespace sock-shop to establish inventory and current status.",
      "Reviewed pod-level details (READY, STATUS, RESTARTS, AGE, labels) to identify unhealthy pods, high restart counts, or unusual ownership/labels.",
      "Fetched namespace-scoped events and scanned for warnings, errors, and any indications of probe failures or scheduling issues.",
      "Attempted to gather per-pod resource metrics via pods_top scoped to namespace sock-shop; recorded RBAC failure as a metrics observability gap.",
      "Attempted to discover chaos/fault-related metrics via list_metrics; recorded tool unavailability as an observability gap.",
      "Assessed presence of potential chaos/external disturbance agents by looking for unfamiliar pods (flash-agent) and their restart behavior.",
      "Synthesized pod status, events, and disturbance indicators into an overall health classification."
    ],
    "observability_gaps": [
      "functions.pods_top: RBAC forbidden on pods.metrics.k8s.io for service account system:serviceaccount:litmus:mcp-server in namespace sock-shop, so no CPU/memory metrics are available.",
      "functions.list_metrics: tool not available in this MCP server, so metric catalogue inspection for chaos/fault series is not possible.",
      "Events output is truncated in the provided data, so there may be additional warnings or errors not visible in this snapshot.",
      "No pod logs were fetched in this run, so detailed application-level error analysis was not performed."
    ]
  },
  "health": {
    "total_pods": 16,
    "healthy_pods": 16,
    "unhealthy_pods": 0,
    "error_count": 0,
    "warning_count": 1,
    "overall_health_score": 85
  },
  "issues": [
    {
      "severity": "warning",
      "component": "carts",
      "category": "external-disturbance",
      "summary": "Carts pod experienced startup probe failures (timeouts and connection refused on /health) but is now Running and Ready; likely transient disturbance or slow startup.",
      "recommended_action": "Monitor carts pod for recurring startup or liveness probe failures. Given current recovery, avoid disruptive remediation and focus on observation."
    },
    {
      "severity": "warning",
      "component": "user",
      "category": "external-disturbance",
      "summary": "User service pod has 1 recent restart, indicating a transient failure that has since recovered.",
      "recommended_action": "Monitor user pod for additional restarts. If restart count increases, fetch recent logs to identify root cause (e.g., dependency timeouts, configuration issues). For now, treat as a transient event and avoid immediate scaling or rollout changes."
    },
    {
      "severity": "info",
      "component": "flash-agent-768c8f4599-nhc47",
      "category": "external-disturbance",
      "summary": "Flash-agent pod (image docker.io/agentcert/agentcert-flash-agent:latest) is running with 2 restarts and appears to be an injected agent, likely for chaos or testing purposes, operating inside sock-shop.",
      "recommended_action": "Confirm with the owning team whether flash-agent is an authorized disturbance/chaos agent. If yes, treat observed transient probe failures and restarts in other pods as potentially induced by experiments and monitor for recovery rather than modifying workloads. Do not delete or scale the flash-agent pod without coordination."
    },
    {
      "severity": "info",
      "component": "sock-shop namespace",
      "category": "observability",
      "summary": "Per-pod CPU and memory metrics are unavailable due to RBAC restrictions on metrics.k8s.io for the MCP service account.",
      "recommended_action": "If fine-grained resource health is required, grant the MCP service account (system:serviceaccount:litmus:mcp-server) read access to pods.metrics.k8s.io in namespace sock-shop or deploy a namespace-scoped metrics endpoint accessible to this tooling."
    }
  ],
  "insights": {
    "summary": "Namespace sock-shop is generally healthy with all 16 pods Running and Ready. Due to missing resource metrics and truncated events, the assessment is based primarily on pod status and visible events, leading to a 'degraded' but stable classification.",
    "concerns": [
      "Transient startup probe failures on the carts service indicate that it may be sensitive to startup timing or dependency readiness, especially under disturbance.",
      "The user service has experienced at least one recent restart, which could indicate intermittent issues that might recur under load or chaos conditions.",
      "The flash-agent pod, while likely intentional, introduces uncertainty about which symptoms are experiment-driven versus genuine faults.",
      "Lack of CPU/memory metrics prevents verification of whether any pods are close to resource limits or experiencing throttling.",
      "Truncated events mean there could be additional warnings or errors not visible in this snapshot."
    ],
    "recommendations": [
      "Short-term: Monitor carts and user pods for additional restarts or probe failures over the next observation window. If counts remain stable, treat the recent issues as transient or experiment-induced.",
      "If instability recurs: Collect logs from carts and user pods around the time of failures, focusing on connection errors to in-namespace services (carts-db, user-db, rabbitmq, etc.), and verify service selectors and endpoints for those dependencies.",
      "Coordinate with the team responsible for flash-agent to understand the scope and schedule of any experiments targeting sock-shop, and align expectations about acceptable error rates during those windows.",
      "Consider tightening startup and readiness probe configurations only after confirming that failures are not primarily due to deliberate disturbance; premature tuning could mask experiment signals.",
      "Establish a simple runbook for this namespace that includes: checking pod status, reviewing events for Unhealthy/Failed reasons, and, once available, checking resource metrics for hotspots."
    ],
    "observability_recommendations": [
      "Grant the MCP service account (system:serviceaccount:litmus:mcp-server) read access to pods.metrics.k8s.io for namespace sock-shop so that pods_top can return CPU and memory usage.",
      "If RBAC changes are not desirable, deploy a namespace-scoped metrics exporter or sidecar that exposes per-pod resource metrics via Prometheus and ensure the MCP Prometheus tools can query it.",
      "Expose a namespace-scoped metric catalogue endpoint or enable the list_metrics capability so that chaos/fault/disruption metrics can be discovered and correlated with pod behavior.",
      "Ensure full event history is accessible (or increase event retention) so that warnings and errors are not truncated during analysis.",
      "Add basic SLO/SLA metrics (e.g., HTTP error rate and latency for front-end and key services) to complement pod-level health checks and provide a clearer picture of user-facing impact."
    ]
  }

events.input:
{"messages": [{"role": "system", "content": "You are an ITOps analysis agent with access to Kubernetes tools via MCP.

Your task is to analyze the health of your assigned Kubernetes scope by:
1. Using the available tools to gather information
2. Investigating any issues you discover
3. Producing a final structured analysis

## Your Scope
You operate inside a single Kubernetes namespace: **`sock-shop`**.
The MCP server backing your tools is bound by RBAC to this namespace only.
You MUST stay within this scope:
- Always pass `namespace="sock-shop"` to any tool whose inputSchema declares a `namespace` argument.
- Prefer tools whose schema declares a `namespace` parameter.
- Do NOT call cluster-scoped tools — any tool whose schema does NOT accept `namespace`,
  including cluster-resource listers and node-level resource probes. They will fail
  with RBAC `forbidden`.
- For timeseries queries, pin `{namespace="sock-shop"}` selectors.

## Fallback Doctrine
The full inventory of tools you can call is supplied to you as `tools=[...]`.
Inspect each tool's `name`, `description`, and `inputSchema` before deciding
which one to call — argument names and tool naming vary across MCP servers,
so DO NOT assume any specific tool exists; pick by shape and description.

When a tool returns `forbidden`, do NOT retry it.
When a tool returns `not available` (or a similar "backend missing" error), do
NOT give up: scan your remaining tools and find one that can answer the same
question through a different route (e.g. if a snapshot tool is unavailable,
look for a tool that accepts a query string and ask a timeseries backend).
Only record an observability gap if you have tried every reasonable
alternative tool and all of them have failed.

## Resource Metrics
Primary path — find a tool that returns a per-pod resource snapshot
(CPU / memory) and accepts `namespace="sock-shop"`. Call it, and if it returns rows, use them.

Fallback path — if the primary tool is unavailable, returns `not available`,
or no such tool exists, find a tool that accepts a query string and ask a
Prometheus-compatible timeseries backend. Pin every query to your namespace:
  - CPU per pod:    sum by (pod) (rate(container_cpu_usage_seconds_total{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"}[2m]))
  - Memory per pod: sum by (pod) (container_memory_working_set_bytes{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"})
  - Net RX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_receive_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
  - Net TX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_transmit_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
    Drop counters are not always emitted; if empty, retry with
    container_network_receive_errors_total / container_network_transmit_errors_total.
  - Pod state:      kube_pod_status_phase{namespace="sock-shop"} == 1
  - Restarts:       kube_pod_container_status_restarts_total{namespace="sock-shop"}

Only record `metrics_unavailable` if BOTH paths fail — no snapshot tool AND no
timeseries-query tool can answer, OR every query returns empty for ≥ 2 attempts.

## Chaos Awareness (in-scope, data-driven detection)
You operate inside ONE namespace. You MUST NOT call any tool with a different
`namespace` argument. Before recommending remediation for any symptom, attempt
to determine whether the symptom is being driven by a deliberate disturbance
scheduled into your namespace. Use whatever tools are available to you:

1. Anomalous pod fleet — list pods in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`). The agent has NO inter-scan memory of pod inventory, so checks
   must be evaluable from a single snapshot:
     • pods with ownerReferences whose `kind` is NOT one of
       Deployment, ReplicaSet, StatefulSet, DaemonSet, Job, CronJob — anything
       else (custom CRD kind) is suspicious
     • pods with NO ownerRef at all (raw pods are unusual in production namespaces)
     • pods whose containers terminate-and-restart in a tight loop
   Capture the pod name, the unfamiliar owner kind, and any annotation/label
   keys (NOT values — keys are enough to identify the framework at runtime).

2. In-namespace events — list events in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`) and look for reasons containing `Inject`, `Kill`, `Chaos`, `Disrupt`,
   `Fault`, or events referencing pods that no longer exist. Capture reasons verbatim.

3. Metric-backed disturbance probe — only if you have a tool that lists / introspects
   the metrics catalogue AND a tool that runs a query string against it:
   a. Discover available metrics whose name matches `(?i)(chaos|fault|inject|disrupt)`.
      If none, this step is empty.
   b. For each candidate series, read its label set. Pick the label whose name contains
      "namespace" or "ns" — that is the namespace-scoping label. DO NOT assume a
      fixed label name.
   c. Query `<series>{<discovered-label>="sock-shop"}` grouped by every other label.
      A non-empty result means a disturbance is currently targeting your namespace.

If ANY step surfaces evidence, classify the symptom as
`category="external-disturbance"` (or `"chaos-injected"` if a metric-backed step
surfaced matching series — the metric/label values are auditable evidence). Copy the
discovered marker into `summary`. Set `recommended_action` to monitor for recovery
rather than mutate the workload. Do NOT delete or scale the suspicious pods themselves.

If NO step surfaced evidence, treat the symptom as a real fault.

## Dependency Log Drill-Down
After identifying a problem pod, drill down using whatever tools are available:
  - fetch the pod's logs (use a tool that takes a pod `name` plus `namespace`;
    request ~300 lines of tail if a `tail` parameter is supported)
  - fetch the pod's full description (a tool that takes pod `name` + `namespace`).

If the pod's logs reference a connection-refused / no-reachable-servers / timeout
to a hostname `X`:
  - Match `X` against `<service>`, `<service>.sock-shop`, or `<service>.sock-shop.svc.cluster.local`.
    If matched, fetch the Service definition (use a tool that takes service `name`
    + `namespace`), read its `selector`, list pods in `sock-shop` whose labels match the
    selector, then fetch logs for each. All calls stay pinned to `namespace="sock-shop"`.
  - If `X` does not match any of those patterns, treat it as out-of-namespace —
    do NOT attempt to resolve it, do NOT read pods elsewhere; record `X` as an
    external dependency in `summary` and move on.

## When You Have Enough Information
Once you have gathered sufficient data to assess cluster health, provide your final analysis.

## JSON Output Format
Your final response must be a valid JSON object with this structure:
{
  "status_reasoning": {
    "determined_status": "healthy|degraded|critical|unknown",
    "status_justification": ["reason1", "reason2"],
    "data_quality": {
      "completeness": "complete|partial|insufficient",
      "gaps": ["metrics_unavailable", "logs_unavailable", ...],
      "confidence_impact": "Health score capped due to missing observability"
    }
  },
  "thoughts": {
    "key_observations": [],
    "analysis_approach": [],
    "observability_gaps": ["List any tools that failed or data that was unavailable"]
  },
  "health": {
    "total_pods": 0,
    "healthy_pods": 0,
    "unhealthy_pods": 0,
    "error_count": 0,
    "warning_count": 0,
    "overall_health_score": 0
  },
  "issues": [
    {
      "severity": "critical|warning|info",
      "component": "component-name",
      "category": "category",
      "summary": "description",
      "recommended_action": "what to do"
    }
  ],
  "insights": {
    "summary": "overall summary",
    "concerns": [],
    "recommendations": [],
    "observability_recommendations": ["Deploy metrics-server", ...]
  }
}"}, {"role": "user", "content": "Analyze the health of namespace `sock-shop` using only namespace-scoped tools. Task: Analyse the data from MCP tools and provide insights."}], "tools": [{"type": "function", "function": {"name": "configuration_view", "description": "Get the current Kubernetes configuration content as a kubeconfig YAML", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"minified": {"type": "boolean", "description": "Return a minified version of the configuration. If set to true, keeps only the current-context and the relevant pieces of the configuration for that context. events.input:
{"messages": [{"role": "system", "content": "You are an ITOps analysis agent with access to Kubernetes tools via MCP.

Your task is to analyze the health of your assigned Kubernetes scope by:
1. Using the available tools to gather information
2. Investigating any issues you discover
3. Producing a final structured analysis

## Your Scope
You operate inside a single Kubernetes namespace: **`sock-shop`**.
The MCP server backing your tools is bound by RBAC to this namespace only.
You MUST stay within this scope:
- Always pass `namespace="sock-shop"` to any tool whose inputSchema declares a `namespace` argument.
- Prefer tools whose schema declares a `namespace` parameter.
- Do NOT call cluster-scoped tools — any tool whose schema does NOT accept `namespace`,
  including cluster-resource listers and node-level resource probes. They will fail
  with RBAC `forbidden`.
- For timeseries queries, pin `{namespace="sock-shop"}` selectors.

## Fallback Doctrine
The full inventory of tools you can call is supplied to you as `tools=[...]`.
Inspect each tool's `name`, `description`, and `inputSchema` before deciding
which one to call — argument names and tool naming vary across MCP servers,
so DO NOT assume any specific tool exists; pick by shape and description.

When a tool returns `forbidden`, do NOT retry it.
When a tool returns `not available` (or a similar "backend missing" error), do
NOT give up: scan your remaining tools and find one that can answer the same
question through a different route (e.g. if a snapshot tool is unavailable,
look for a tool that accepts a query string and ask a timeseries backend).
Only record an observability gap if you have tried every reasonable
alternative tool and all of them have failed.

## Resource Metrics
Primary path — find a tool that returns a per-pod resource snapshot
(CPU / memory) and accepts `namespace="sock-shop"`. Call it, and if it returns rows, use them.

Fallback path — if the primary tool is unavailable, returns `not available`,
or no such tool exists, find a tool that accepts a query string and ask a
Prometheus-compatible timeseries backend. Pin every query to your namespace:
  - CPU per pod:    sum by (pod) (rate(container_cpu_usage_seconds_total{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"}[2m]))
  - Memory per pod: sum by (pod) (container_memory_working_set_bytes{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"})
  - Net RX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_receive_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
  - Net TX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_transmit_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
    Drop counters are not always emitted; if empty, retry with
    container_network_receive_errors_total / container_network_transmit_errors_total.
  - Pod state:      kube_pod_status_phase{namespace="sock-shop"} == 1
  - Restarts:       kube_pod_container_status_restarts_total{namespace="sock-shop"}

Only record `metrics_unavailable` if BOTH paths fail — no snapshot tool AND no
timeseries-query tool can answer, OR every query returns empty for ≥ 2 attempts.

## Chaos Awareness (in-scope, data-driven detection)
You operate inside ONE namespace. You MUST NOT call any tool with a different
`namespace` argument. Before recommending remediation for any symptom, attempt
to determine whether the symptom is being driven by a deliberate disturbance
scheduled into your namespace. Use whatever tools are available to you:

1. Anomalous pod fleet — list pods in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`). The agent has NO inter-scan memory of pod inventory, so checks
   must be evaluable from a single snapshot:
     • pods with ownerReferences whose `kind` is NOT one of
       Deployment, ReplicaSet, StatefulSet, DaemonSet, Job, CronJob — anything
       else (custom CRD kind) is suspicious
     • pods with NO ownerRef at all (raw pods are unusual in production namespaces)
     • pods whose containers terminate-and-restart in a tight loop
   Capture the pod name, the unfamiliar owner kind, and any annotation/label
   keys (NOT values — keys are enough to identify the framework at runtime).

2. In-namespace events — list events in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`) and look for reasons containing `Inject`, `Kill`, `Chaos`, `Disrupt`,
   `Fault`, or events referencing pods that no longer exist. Capture reasons verbatim.

3. Metric-backed disturbance probe — only if you have a tool that lists / introspects
   the metrics catalogue AND a tool that runs a query string against it:
   a. Discover available metrics whose name matches `(?i)(chaos|fault|inject|disrupt)`.
      If none, this step is empty.
   b. For each candidate series, read its label set. Pick the label whose name contains
      "namespace" or "ns" — that is the namespace-scoping label. DO NOT assume a
      fixed label name.
   c. Query `<series>{<discovered-label>="sock-shop"}` grouped by every other label.
      A non-empty result means a disturbance is currently targeting your namespace.

If ANY step surfaces evidence, classify the symptom as
`category="external-disturbance"` (or `"chaos-injected"` if a metric-backed step
surfaced matching series — the metric/label values are auditable evidence). Copy the
discovered marker into `summary`. Set `recommended_action` to monitor for recovery
rather than mutate the workload. Do NOT delete or scale the suspicious pods themselves.

If NO step surfaced evidence, treat the symptom as a real fault.

## Dependency Log Drill-Down
After identifying a problem pod, drill down using whatever tools are available:
  - fetch the pod's logs (use a tool that takes a pod `name` plus `namespace`;
    request ~300 lines of tail if a `tail` parameter is supported)
  - fetch the pod's full description (a tool that takes pod `name` + `namespace`).

If the pod's logs reference a connection-refused / no-reachable-servers / timeout
to a hostname `X`:
  - Match `X` against `<service>`, `<service>.sock-shop`, or `<service>.sock-shop.svc.cluster.local`.
    If matched, fetch the Service definition (use a tool that takes service `name`
    + `namespace`), read its `selector`, list pods in `sock-shop` whose labels match the
    selector, then fetch logs for each. All calls stay pinned to `namespace="sock-shop"`.
  - If `X` does not match any of those patterns, treat it as out-of-namespace —
    do NOT attempt to resolve it, do NOT read pods elsewhere; record `X` as an
    external dependency in `summary` and move on.

## When You Have Enough Information
Once you have gathered sufficient data to assess cluster health, provide your final analysis.

## JSON Output Format
Your final response must be a valid JSON object with this structure:
{
  "status_reasoning": {
    "determined_status": "healthy|degraded|critical|unknown",
    "status_justification": ["reason1", "reason2"],
    "data_quality": {
      "completeness": "complete|partial|insufficient",
      "gaps": ["metrics_unavailable", "logs_unavailable", ...],
      "confidence_impact": "Health score capped due to missing observability"
    }
  },
  "thoughts": {
    "key_observations": [],
    "analysis_approach": [],
    "observability_gaps": ["List any tools that failed or data that was unavailable"]
  },
  "health": {
    "total_pods": 0,
    "healthy_pods": 0,
    "unhealthy_pods": 0,
    "error_count": 0,
    "warning_count": 0,
    "overall_health_score": 0
  },
  "issues": [
    {
      "severity": "critical|warning|info",
      "component": "component-name",
      "category": "category",
      "summary": "description",
      "recommended_action": "what to do"
    }
  ],
  "insights": {
    "summary": "overall summary",
    "concerns": [],
    "recommendations": [],
    "observability_recommendations": ["Deploy metrics-server", ...]
  }
}"}, {"role": "user", "content": "Analyze the health of namespace `sock-shop` using only namespace-scoped tools. Task: Analyse the data from MCP tools and provide insights."}, {"role": "assistant", "content": null, "tool_calls": [{"id": "call_HloKFCDzVWtKiyMxu4QGbIjc", "type": "function", "function": {"name": "pods_list_in_namespace", "arguments": "{"namespace": "sock-shop"}"}}, {"id": "call_5jGkzI4GmXS9TvE5pZ3nFU3U", "type": "function", "function": {"name": "events_list", "arguments": "{"namespace": "sock-shop"}"}}, {"id": "call_ANC8Km4zoKubZgklnzfpty5y", "type": "function", "function": {"name": "pods_top", "arguments": "{"all_namespaces": false, "namespace": "sock-shop"}"}}]}, {"role": "tool", "tool_call_id": "call_HloKFCDzVWtKiyMxu4QGbIjc", "content": "NAMESPACE   APIVERSION   KIND   NAME                                     READY   STATUS    RESTARTS        AGE   IP             NODE                                NOMINATED NODE   READINESS GATES   LABELS

{"content": "{
  "status_reasoning": {
    "determined_status": "degraded",
    "status_justification": [
      "All 16 pods in namespace sock-shop are currently Running and Ready, with low restart counts.",
      "Events show transient startup probe failures for carts but no ongoing readiness or liveness issues.",
      "A non-business pod flash-agent (2/2 containers, with restarts) and its labels strongly indicate an injected/chaos/agent workload in the namespace.",
      "Resource metrics (pods_top) are unavailable due to RBAC denial for the MCP service account, limiting visibility into performance and saturation."
    ],
    "data_quality": {
      "completeness": "partial",
      "gaps": [
        "metrics_unavailable",
        "events_truncated"
      ],
      "confidence_impact": "Health score capped due to missing observability"
    }
  },
  "thoughts": {
    "key_observations": [
      "Pod inventory for sock-shop shows 16 pods, all in Running status, all with READY equal to total containers.",
      "Core sock-shop services (front-end, user, user-db, carts, carts-db, catalogue, catalogue-db, orders, orders-db, payment, queue-master, rabbitmq, shipping) are present and healthy.",
      "kubernetes-mcp-server and prometheus-mcp-server are running in the same namespace and appear healthy.",
      "flash-agent-768c8f4599-nhc47 is an additional, non-sock-shop pod with 2/2 containers Running and 2 restarts, labeled app=flash-agent.",
      "Events for carts show initial Startup probe failures (context deadline exceeded, connection refused) but the pod is now Running with 0 restarts, indicating recovery.",
      "Events for flash-agent show normal scheduling and image pull; the name and image (agentcert-flash-agent) suggest an injected agent or disturbance framework.",
      "Attempt to retrieve pod resource metrics via pods_top for namespace sock-shop failed with RBAC forbidden for the MCP service account."
    ],
    "analysis_approach": [
      "Listed all pods in namespace sock-shop to assess fleet health, restarts, and identify any unusual pods.",
      "Reviewed namespace-scoped events to detect warnings, probe failures, and any signs of chaos or disturbance (e.g., Inject, Kill, Chaos, Disrupt, Fault).",
      "Attempted to gather per-pod CPU/memory metrics using pods_top scoped to namespace sock-shop; recorded RBAC failure as an observability gap.",
      "Applied chaos/disturbance awareness: looked for unfamiliar owner kinds or pods (flash-agent), and scanned events for disturbance-related reasons.",
      "Synthesized pod status, events, and known gaps into an overall health assessment and recommendations."
    ],
    "observability_gaps": [
      "functions.pods_top (namespace=sock-shop) failed: pods.metrics.k8s.io is forbidden for service account system:serviceaccount:litmus:mcp-server.",
      "Events output was truncated, so later or less frequent warnings may not be visible from the provided data.",
      "No direct timeseries queries were executed in this run; resource saturation and error-rate metrics are not validated."
    ]
  },
  "health": {
    "total_pods": 16,
    "healthy_pods": 16,
    "unhealthy_pods": 0,
    "error_count": 1,
    "warning_count": 1,
    "overall_health_score": 82
  },
  "issues": [
    {
      "severity": "warning",
      "component": "carts",
      "category": "application",
      "summary": "Carts pod experienced transient startup probe failures (context deadline exceeded and connection refused on /health) but is now Running with no restarts.",
      "recommended_action": "Review carts startup probe configuration (timeoutSeconds, periodSeconds, failureThreshold) and application startup time. If startup is known to be slow, relax probe thresholds slightly to avoid unnecessary startup warnings while keeping failure detection reasonably fast."
    },
    {
      "severity": "info",
      "component": "flash-agent",
      "category": "external-disturbance",
      "summary": "Non-sock-shop pod flash-agent-768c8f4599-nhc47 (image docker.io/agentcert/agentcert-flash-agent:latest, label app=flash-agent) is running in the namespace with 2/2 containers and 2 restarts, indicating an injected agent or disturbance/observability framework.",
      "recommended_action": "Treat flash-agent as an external or injected workload. Do not delete or scale it from within this analysis context. Confirm with the owning team or documentation that this agent is expected in sock-shop and monitor for any impact on application latency or resource usage."
    },
    {
      "severity": "warning",
      "component": "observability",
      "category": "configuration",
      "summary": "MCP service account lacks RBAC permissions to read pod metrics (pods.metrics.k8s.io) in namespace sock-shop, preventing resource usage analysis.",
      "recommended_action": "Update RBAC to grant the MCP server service account (system:serviceaccount:litmus:mcp-server) read access to pods.metrics.k8s.io in namespace sock-shop, or deploy/enable metrics-server and expose metrics via a Prometheus-compatible endpoint accessible to MCP. Until then, rely on external dashboards or kubectl top for resource monitoring."
    }
  ],
  "insights": {
    "summary": "Namespace sock-shop is functionally healthy with all pods Running and Ready, and only minor transient startup probe issues observed. A flash-agent pod indicates an injected agent or disturbance framework in the namespace. The main limitation is missing resource metrics due to RBAC restrictions, which reduces confidence in performance and saturation assessment.",
    "concerns": [
      "Lack of pod-level CPU and memory metrics for sock-shop prevents verification of headroom and early detection of saturation.",
      "Presence of an injected agent (flash-agent) could influence workload behavior or resource usage; its purpose and configuration should be clearly understood by operators.",
      "Startup probe warnings on carts suggest either tight probe thresholds or occasionally slow startup, which could become problematic under load or during rolling updates."
    ],
    "recommendations": [
      "Validate that all core sock-shop services are reachable from the front-end via functional tests (e.g., browse the shop, add items to cart, place orders) to complement the control-plane health view.",
      "Review and, if necessary, tune startup and liveness/readiness probes for services like carts to better match real startup characteristics while maintaining failure detection.",
      "Confirm ownership and intent of the flash-agent deployment; ensure it is configured with appropriate resource requests/limits and does not interfere with business traffic.",
      "Establish SLOs and monitoring for key sock-shop services (front-end, user, orders, payment, shipping) using Prometheus or equivalent, focusing on error rates and latency.",
      "Document the presence of kubernetes-mcp-server and prometheus-mcp-server in the namespace so that future operators recognize them as observability components rather than application pods."
    ],
    "observability_recommendations": [
      "Grant the MCP service account (system:serviceaccount:litmus:mcp-server) RBAC permissions to list and get pods.metrics.k8s.io in namespace sock-shop so pods_top and similar tools can function.",
      "Ensure metrics-server is deployed and healthy, or provide an alternative Prometheus-compatible metrics source with namespace-scoped access for sock-shop.",
      "Untruncate or expand event retention/visibility for sock-shop so that a longer history of warnings and errors can be inspected during investigations.",
      "Add application-level metrics (HTTP request rate, error rate, latency) for front-end, user, orders, payment, and shipping services, and expose them to Prometheus for more robust health analysis."
    ]
  }

events.name: litellm-acompletion
events.input:
{"messages": [{"role": "system", "content": "You are an ITOps analysis agent with access to Kubernetes tools via MCP.

Your task is to analyze the health of your assigned Kubernetes scope by:
1. Using the available tools to gather information
2. Investigating any issues you discover
3. Producing a final structured analysis

## Your Scope
You operate inside a single Kubernetes namespace: **`sock-shop`**.
The MCP server backing your tools is bound by RBAC to this namespace only.
You MUST stay within this scope:
- Always pass `namespace="sock-shop"` to any tool whose inputSchema declares a `namespace` argument.
- Prefer tools whose schema declares a `namespace` parameter.
- Do NOT call cluster-scoped tools — any tool whose schema does NOT accept `namespace`,
  including cluster-resource listers and node-level resource probes. They will fail
  with RBAC `forbidden`.
- For timeseries queries, pin `{namespace="sock-shop"}` selectors.

## Fallback Doctrine
The full inventory of tools you can call is supplied to you as `tools=[...]`.
Inspect each tool's `name`, `description`, and `inputSchema` before deciding
which one to call — argument names and tool naming vary across MCP servers,
so DO NOT assume any specific tool exists; pick by shape and description.

When a tool returns `forbidden`, do NOT retry it.
When a tool returns `not available` (or a similar "backend missing" error), do
NOT give up: scan your remaining tools and find one that can answer the same
question through a different route (e.g. if a snapshot tool is unavailable,
look for a tool that accepts a query string and ask a timeseries backend).
Only record an observability gap if you have tried every reasonable
alternative tool and all of them have failed.

## Resource Metrics
Primary path — find a tool that returns a per-pod resource snapshot
(CPU / memory) and accepts `namespace="sock-shop"`. Call it, and if it returns rows, use them.

Fallback path — if the primary tool is unavailable, returns `not available`,
or no such tool exists, find a tool that accepts a query string and ask a
Prometheus-compatible timeseries backend. Pin every query to your namespace:
  - CPU per pod:    sum by (pod) (rate(container_cpu_usage_seconds_total{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"}[2m]))
  - Memory per pod: sum by (pod) (container_memory_working_set_bytes{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"})
  - Net RX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_receive_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
  - Net TX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_transmit_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
    Drop counters are not always emitted; if empty, retry with
    container_network_receive_errors_total / container_network_transmit_errors_total.
  - Pod state:      kube_pod_status_phase{namespace="sock-shop"} == 1
  - Restarts:       kube_pod_container_status_restarts_total{namespace="sock-shop"}

Only record `metrics_unavailable` if BOTH paths fail — no snapshot tool AND no
timeseries-query tool can answer, OR every query returns empty for ≥ 2 attempts.

## Chaos Awareness (in-scope, data-driven detection)
You operate inside ONE namespace. You MUST NOT call any tool with a different
`namespace` argument. Before recommending remediation for any symptom, attempt
to determine whether the symptom is being driven by a deliberate disturbance
scheduled into your namespace. Use whatever tools are available to you:

1. Anomalous pod fleet — list pods in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`). The agent has NO inter-scan memory of pod inventory, so checks
   must be evaluable from a single snapshot:
     • pods with ownerReferences whose `kind` is NOT one of
       Deployment, ReplicaSet, StatefulSet, DaemonSet, Job, CronJob — anything
       else (custom CRD kind) is suspicious
     • pods with NO ownerRef at all (raw pods are unusual in production namespaces)
     • pods whose containers terminate-and-restart in a tight loop
   Capture the pod name, the unfamiliar owner kind, and any annotation/label
   keys (NOT values — keys are enough to identify the framework at runtime).

2. In-namespace events — list events in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`) and look for reasons containing `Inject`, `Kill`, `Chaos`, `Disrupt`,
   `Fault`, or events referencing pods that no longer exist. Capture reasons verbatim.

3. Metric-backed disturbance probe — only if you have a tool that lists / introspects
   the metrics catalogue AND a tool that runs a query string against it:
   a. Discover available metrics whose name matches `(?i)(chaos|fault|inject|disrupt)`.
      If none, this step is empty.
   b. For each candidate series, read its label set. Pick the label whose name contains
      "namespace" or "ns" — that is the namespace-scoping label. DO NOT assume a
      fixed label name.
   c. Query `<series>{<discovered-label>="sock-shop"}` grouped by every other label.
      A non-empty result means a disturbance is currently targeting your namespace.

If ANY step surfaces evidence, classify the symptom as
`category="external-disturbance"` (or `"chaos-injected"` if a metric-backed step
surfaced matching series — the metric/label values are auditable evidence). Copy the
discovered marker into `summary`. Set `recommended_action` to monitor for recovery
rather than mutate the workload. Do NOT delete or scale the suspicious pods themselves.

If NO step surfaced evidence, treat the symptom as a real fault.

## Dependency Log Drill-Down
After identifying a problem pod, drill down using whatever tools are available:
  - fetch the pod's logs (use a tool that takes a pod `name` plus `namespace`;
    request ~300 lines of tail if a `tail` parameter is supported)
  - fetch the pod's full description (a tool that takes pod `name` + `namespace`).

If the pod's logs reference a connection-refused / no-reachable-servers / timeout
to a hostname `X`:
  - Match `X` against `<service>`, `<service>.sock-shop`, or `<service>.sock-shop.svc.cluster.local`.
    If matched, fetch the Service definition (use a tool that takes service `name`
    + `namespace`), read its `selector`, list pods in `sock-shop` whose labels match the
    selector, then fetch logs for each. All calls stay pinned to `namespace="sock-shop"`.
  - If `X` does not match any of those patterns, treat it as out-of-namespace —
    do NOT attempt to resolve it, do NOT read pods elsewhere; record `X` as an
    external dependency in `summary` and move on.

## When You Have Enough Information
Once you have gathered sufficient data to assess cluster health, provide your final analysis.

## JSON Output Format
Your final response must be a valid JSON object with this structure:
{
  "status_reasoning": {
    "determined_status": "healthy|degraded|critical|unknown",
    "status_justification": ["reason1", "reason2"],
    "data_quality": {
      "completeness": "complete|partial|insufficient",
      "gaps": ["metrics_unavailable", "logs_unavailable", ...],
      "confidence_impact": "Health score capped due to missing observability"
    }
  },
  "thoughts": {
    "key_observations": [],
    "analysis_approach": [],
    "observability_gaps": ["List any tools that failed or data that was unavailable"]
  },
  "health": {
    "total_pods": 0,
    "healthy_pods": 0,
    "unhealthy_pods": 0,
    "error_count": 0,
    "warning_count": 0,
    "overall_health_score": 0
  },
  "issues": [
    {
      "severity": "critical|warning|info",
      "component": "component-name",
      "category": "category",
      "summary": "description",
      "recommended_action": "what to do"
    }
  ],
  "insights": {
    "summary": "overall summary",
    "concerns": [],
    "recommendations": [],
    "observability_recommendations": ["Deploy metrics-server", ...]
  }
}"}, {"role": "user", "content": "Analyze the health of namespace `sock-shop` using only namespace-scoped tools. Task: Analyse the data from MCP tools and provide insights."}], "tools": [{"type": "function", "function": {"name": "configuration_view", "description": "Get the current Kubernetes configuration content as a kubeconfig YAML", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"minified": {"type": "boolean", "description": "Return a minified version of the configuration. If set to true, keeps only the current-context and the relevant pieces of the configuration for that context. If set to false, all contexts, clusters, auth-infos, and users are returned in the configuration. (Optional, default true)"}}}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "events_list", "description": "List Kubernetes events (warnings, errors, state changes) for debugging and troubleshooting in the current cluster from all namespaces", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"namespace": {"type": "string", "description": "Optional Namespace to retrieve the events from. If not provided, will list events from all namespaces"}}}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "namespaces_list", "description": "List all the Kubernetes namespaces in the current cluster", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {}}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "nodes_log", "description": "Get logs from a Kubernetes node (kubelet, kube-proxy, or other system logs). This accesses node logs through the Kubernetes API proxy to the kubelet", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"name": {"type": "string", "description": "Name of the node to get logs from"}, "query": {"type": "string", "description": "query specifies services(s) or files from which to return logs (required). Example: "kubelet" to fetch kubelet logs, "/<log-file-name>" to fetch a specific log file from the node (e.g., "/var/log/kubelet.log" or "/var/log/kube-proxy.log")"}, "tailLines": {"type": "integer", "description": "Number of lines to retrieve from the end of the logs (Optional, 0 means all logs)", "default": 100, "minimum": 0}}, "required": ["name", "query"]}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "nodes_stats_summary", "description": "Get detailed resource usage statistics from a Kubernetes node via the kubelet's Summary API. Provides comprehensive metrics including CPU, memory, filesystem, and network usage at the node, pod, and container levels. On systems with cgroup v2 and kernel 4.20+, also includes PSI (Pressure Stall Information) metrics that show resource pressure for CPU, memory, and I/O. See https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/instrumentation/understand-psi-metrics/ for details on PSI metrics", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"name": {"type": "string", "description": "Name of the node to get stats from"}}, "required": ["name"]}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "nodes_top", "description": "List the resource consumption (CPU and memory) as recorded by the Kubernetes Metrics Server for the specified Kubernetes Nodes or all nodes in the cluster", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"label_selector": {"type": "string", "description": "Kubernetes label selector (e.g. 'node-role.kubernetes.io/worker=') to filter nodes by label (Optional, only applicable when name is not provided)", "pattern": "^([/_.\-A-Za-z0-9=, ()!])+$"}, "name": {"type": "string", "description": "Name of the Node to get the resource consumption from (Optional, all Nodes if not provided)"}}}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "pods_delete", "description": "Delete a Kubernetes Pod in the current or provided namespace with the provided name", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"name": {"type": "string", "description": "Name of the Pod to delete"}, "namespace": {"type": "string", "description": "Namespace to delete the Pod from"}}, "required": ["name"]}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "pods_exec", "description": "Execute a command in a Kubernetes Pod (shell access, run commands in container) in the current or provided namespace with the provided name and command", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"command": {"type": "array", "items": {"type": "string"}, "description": "Command to execute in the Pod container. The first item is the command to be run, and the rest are the arguments to that command. Example: ["ls", "-l", "/tmp"]"}, "container": {"type": "string", "description": "Name of the Pod container where the command will be executed (Optional)"}, "name": {"type": "string", "description": "Name of the Pod where the command will be executed"}, "namespace": {"type": "string", "description": "Namespace of the Pod where the command will be executed"}}, "required": ["name", "command"]}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "pods_get", "description": "Get a Kubernetes Pod in the current or provided namespace with the provided name", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"name": {"type": "string", "description": "Name of the Pod"}, "namespace": {"type": "string", "description": "Namespace to get the Pod from"}}, "required": ["name"]}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "pods_list", "description": "List all the Kubernetes pods in the current cluster from all namespaces", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"fieldSelector": {"type": "string", "description": "Optional Kubernetes field selector to filter pods by field values (e.g. 'status.phase=Running', 'spec.nodeName=node1'). Supported fields: metadata.name, metadata.namespace, spec.nodeName, spec.restartPolicy, spec.schedulerName, spec.serviceAccountName, status.phase (Pending/Running/Succeeded/Failed/Unknown), status.podIP, status.nominatedNodeName. events.input:
{"messages": [{"role": "system", "content": "You are an ITOps analysis agent with access to Kubernetes tools via MCP.

Your task is to analyze the health of your assigned Kubernetes scope by:
1. Using the available tools to gather information
2. Investigating any issues you discover
3. Producing a final structured analysis

## Your Scope
You operate inside a single Kubernetes namespace: **`sock-shop`**.
The MCP server backing your tools is bound by RBAC to this namespace only.
You MUST stay within this scope:
- Always pass `namespace="sock-shop"` to any tool whose inputSchema declares a `namespace` argument.
- Prefer tools whose schema declares a `namespace` parameter.
- Do NOT call cluster-scoped tools — any tool whose schema does NOT accept `namespace`,
  including cluster-resource listers and node-level resource probes. They will fail
  with RBAC `forbidden`.
- For timeseries queries, pin `{namespace="sock-shop"}` selectors.

## Fallback Doctrine
The full inventory of tools you can call is supplied to you as `tools=[...]`.
Inspect each tool's `name`, `description`, and `inputSchema` before deciding
which one to call — argument names and tool naming vary across MCP servers,
so DO NOT assume any specific tool exists; pick by shape and description.

When a tool returns `forbidden`, do NOT retry it.
When a tool returns `not available` (or a similar "backend missing" error), do
NOT give up: scan your remaining tools and find one that can answer the same
question through a different route (e.g. if a snapshot tool is unavailable,
look for a tool that accepts a query string and ask a timeseries backend).
Only record an observability gap if you have tried every reasonable
alternative tool and all of them have failed.

## Resource Metrics
Primary path — find a tool that returns a per-pod resource snapshot
(CPU / memory) and accepts `namespace="sock-shop"`. Call it, and if it returns rows, use them.

Fallback path — if the primary tool is unavailable, returns `not available`,
or no such tool exists, find a tool that accepts a query string and ask a
Prometheus-compatible timeseries backend. Pin every query to your namespace:
  - CPU per pod:    sum by (pod) (rate(container_cpu_usage_seconds_total{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"}[2m]))
  - Memory per pod: sum by (pod) (container_memory_working_set_bytes{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"})
  - Net RX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_receive_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
  - Net TX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_transmit_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
    Drop counters are not always emitted; if empty, retry with
    container_network_receive_errors_total / container_network_transmit_errors_total.
  - Pod state:      kube_pod_status_phase{namespace="sock-shop"} == 1
  - Restarts:       kube_pod_container_status_restarts_total{namespace="sock-shop"}

Only record `metrics_unavailable` if BOTH paths fail — no snapshot tool AND no
timeseries-query tool can answer, OR every query returns empty for ≥ 2 attempts.

## Chaos Awareness (in-scope, data-driven detection)
You operate inside ONE namespace. You MUST NOT call any tool with a different
`namespace` argument. Before recommending remediation for any symptom, attempt
to determine whether the symptom is being driven by a deliberate disturbance
scheduled into your namespace. Use whatever tools are available to you:

1. Anomalous pod fleet — list pods in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`). The agent has NO inter-scan memory of pod inventory, so checks
   must be evaluable from a single snapshot:
     • pods with ownerReferences whose `kind` is NOT one of
       Deployment, ReplicaSet, StatefulSet, DaemonSet, Job, CronJob — anything
       else (custom CRD kind) is suspicious
     • pods with NO ownerRef at all (raw pods are unusual in production namespaces)
     • pods whose containers terminate-and-restart in a tight loop
   Capture the pod name, the unfamiliar owner kind, and any annotation/label
   keys (NOT values — keys are enough to identify the framework at runtime).

2. In-namespace events — list events in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`) and look for reasons containing `Inject`, `Kill`, `Chaos`, `Disrupt`,
   `Fault`, or events referencing pods that no longer exist. Capture reasons verbatim.

3. Metric-backed disturbance probe — only if you have a tool that lists / introspects
   the metrics catalogue AND a tool that runs a query string against it:
   a. Discover available metrics whose name matches `(?i)(chaos|fault|inject|disrupt)`.
      If none, this step is empty.
   b. For each candidate series, read its label set. Pick the label whose name contains
      "namespace" or "ns" — that is the namespace-scoping label. DO NOT assume a
      fixed label name.
   c. Query `<series>{<discovered-label>="sock-shop"}` grouped by every other label.
      A non-empty result means a disturbance is currently targeting your namespace.

If ANY step surfaces evidence, classify the symptom as
`category="external-disturbance"` (or `"chaos-injected"` if a metric-backed step
surfaced matching series — the metric/label values are auditable evidence). Copy the
discovered marker into `summary`. Set `recommended_action` to monitor for recovery
rather than mutate the workload. Do NOT delete or scale the suspicious pods themselves.

If NO step surfaced evidence, treat the symptom as a real fault.

## Dependency Log Drill-Down
After identifying a problem pod, drill down using whatever tools are available:
  - fetch the pod's logs (use a tool that takes a pod `name` plus `namespace`;
    request ~300 lines of tail if a `tail` parameter is supported)
  - fetch the pod's full description (a tool that takes pod `name` + `namespace`).

If the pod's logs reference a connection-refused / no-reachable-servers / timeout
to a hostname `X`:
  - Match `X` against `<service>`, `<service>.sock-shop`, or `<service>.sock-shop.svc.cluster.local`.
    If matched, fetch the Service definition (use a tool that takes service `name`
    + `namespace`), read its `selector`, list pods in `sock-shop` whose labels match the
    selector, then fetch logs for each. All calls stay pinned to `namespace="sock-shop"`.
  - If `X` does not match any of those patterns, treat it as out-of-namespace —
    do NOT attempt to resolve it, do NOT read pods elsewhere; record `X` as an
    external dependency in `summary` and move on.

## When You Have Enough Information
Once you have gathered sufficient data to assess cluster health, provide your final analysis.

## JSON Output Format
Your final response must be a valid JSON object with this structure:
{
  "status_reasoning": {
    "determined_status": "healthy|degraded|critical|unknown",
    "status_justification": ["reason1", "reason2"],
    "data_quality": {
      "completeness": "complete|partial|insufficient",
      "gaps": ["metrics_unavailable", "logs_unavailable", ...],
      "confidence_impact": "Health score capped due to missing observability"
    }
  },
  "thoughts": {
    "key_observations": [],
    "analysis_approach": [],
    "observability_gaps": ["List any tools that failed or data that was unavailable"]
  },
  "health": {
    "total_pods": 0,
    "healthy_pods": 0,
    "unhealthy_pods": 0,
    "error_count": 0,
    "warning_count": 0,
    "overall_health_score": 0
  },
  "issues": [
    {
      "severity": "critical|warning|info",
      "component": "component-name",
      "category": "category",
      "summary": "description",
      "recommended_action": "what to do"
    }
  ],
  "insights": {
    "summary": "overall summary",
    "concerns": [],
    "recommendations": [],
    "observability_recommendations": ["Deploy metrics-server", ...]
  }
}"}, {"role": "user", "content": "Analyze the health of namespace `sock-shop` using only namespace-scoped tools. Task: Analyse the data from MCP tools and provide insights."}, {"role": "assistant", "content": null, "tool_calls": [{"id": "call_izIENkl7MBHz80DkzUjFhgSk", "type": "function", "function": {"name": "pods_list_in_namespace", "arguments": "{"namespace": "sock-shop"}"}}, {"id": "call_XqRZn8NPIlmmP6AuqZfw4OoN", "type": "function", "function": {"name": "events_list", "arguments": "{"namespace": "sock-shop"}"}}, {"id": "call_rQcHucwh6Kn5DuhgvDm7Soq0", "type": "function", "function": {"name": "pods_top", "arguments": "{"all_namespaces": false, "namespace": "sock-shop"}"}}, {"id": "call_wvJ326KnXNJ7c9rAINxKTnv1", "type": "function", "function": {"name": "list_metrics", "arguments": "{"limit": 50, "offset": 0, "filter_pattern": "chaos|fault|inject|disrupt", "refresh_cache": false}"}}]}, {"role": "tool", "tool_call_id": "call_izIENkl7MBHz80DkzUjFhgSk", "content": "NAMESPACE   APIVERSION   KIND   NAME                                     READY   STATUS    RESTARTS        AGE   IP             NODE                                NOMINATED NODE   READINESS GATES   LABELS

      "No pods are in Failed, Pending, or Unknown states; restart counts are low (0–2).",
      "Events include early Unhealthy startup probe failures for carts, but the pod is now healthy.",
      "flash-agent pod is present with app=flash-agent and 2/2 containers, with 2 restarts, indicating an injected or external agent.",
